title: Civil Society Protests: The US Civil Rights and Black Power Movements
subject: History

meta.title: Civil Society Protests: The US Civil Rights and Black Power Movements
meta.subject: History

sources.title: Grade 12 - Forms of civil society protest emerged from the 1960s to 1970s
1960s: The Civil Rights and Black Power movements
"I Have A Dream" by Martin Luther King
Appeal for action against Apartheid - Statement issued jointly by Chief Albert Luthuli and Reverend Martin Luther King, Jr, 10 December 1962
Letter From Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., to Chief Albert J. Luthuli, 8 December 1959
The Ideology of the Black Consciousness Movement

slides.heading: Civil Society Protests: The US Civil Rights and Black Power Movements
slides.lead: How ordinary citizens in the United States confronted segregation between the 1950s and 1970s - and what their struggle shared with South Africa's.
slides.notes: Open by defining civil society: organised groups of citizens - churches, student bodies, unions, community organisations - acting independently of the state to demand change. This unit examines two linked American movements: the Civil Rights Movement (roughly 1954-1965) and the Black Power Movement that grew out of it. Frame the enduring question for the lesson: when peaceful, law-abiding protest is met with violence and delay, how do movements respond? Signal that we will keep drawing comparisons with the anti-apartheid struggle learners already know.

media.alt: Two raised fists - one open in an outstretched hand of peaceful appeal, one clenched - against a divided background.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='160' width='160' height='180' fill='#e9e0d1'/><g fill='#3a3a3a'><rect x='70' y='96' width='16' height='60' rx='6'/><path d='M62 100 q16 -34 32 0 q-6 8 -16 8 q-10 0 -16 -8 Z'/></g><g fill='#8a1f1f'><rect x='234' y='96' width='16' height='60' rx='6'/><rect x='224' y='78' width='36' height='26' rx='8'/><rect x='224' y='70' width='8' height='16' rx='3'/><rect x='234' y='66' width='8' height='20' rx='3'/><rect x='244' y='70' width='8' height='16' rx='3'/></g><rect x='158' width='4' height='180' fill='#c8b89a'/></svg>

slides.heading: Segregation in the United States
slides.lead: Nearly a century after slavery ended, 'Jim Crow' laws kept black Americans separate and unequal.

blocks.text: Slavery was abolished in 1865, but from the 1870s many Southern states passed **Jim Crow laws** that enforced racial segregation in schools, transport, restaurants and public spaces. The 1896 Supreme Court case *Plessy v. Ferguson* allowed 'separate but equal' facilities - in practice separate and far from equal.

items: Black citizens faced literacy tests and poll taxes designed to deny them the vote.
Racial violence, including lynching, was used to enforce white supremacy.
Comparison: like South Africa's segregation before 1948, US racism was legal, systematic and everyday.

slides.notes: Draw the explicit parallel with South Africa: 'separate but equal' in the US resembled the logic apartheid would later formalise. Stress that segregation in the American South was upheld by law (de jure) in the South and by custom (de facto) in the North. This background explains why the movement's first target was the legal machinery of segregation. Note that the US had a written constitution and courts, which gave activists a legal avenue South Africans largely lacked.

slides.heading: Brown v. Board of Education
slides.lead: On 17 May 1954 the US Supreme Court ruled that segregated public schools were unconstitutional.

blocks.text: The **National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP)**, using lawyers such as **Thurgood Marshall**, challenged school segregation in court. In *Brown v. Board of Education* the Court unanimously overturned 'separate but equal' in education, ruling that segregated schooling was inherently unequal.

items: The ruling gave the movement legal momentum and moral authority.
Many Southern states resisted, and desegregation was slow and bitterly contested.
In 1957 federal troops were sent to Little Rock, Arkansas, to protect nine black students entering a white high school.

media.alt: A judge's gavel resting on a book, with a schoolhouse in the background.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='138' y='40' width='58' height='40' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M134 40 L167 16 L200 40 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='158' y='58' width='16' height='22' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='30' y='120' width='120' height='10' rx='2' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='36' y='108' width='108' height='12' rx='2' fill='#9a7550'/><g fill='#5c3a1e'><rect x='60' y='70' width='14' height='50' rx='4' transform='rotate(38 67 95)'/><rect x='84' y='60' width='40' height='20' rx='6' transform='rotate(38 104 70)'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Highlight that the movement worked on two fronts at once: the courts and the streets. Brown was a legal victory, but a court ruling alone did not change daily life - white resistance ('massive resistance') showed that laws needed mass action behind them. Little Rock is a vivid example of how far the state had to go to enforce a court order. This tension between legal and mass strategies runs through the whole unit.

slides.heading: The Montgomery Bus Boycott
slides.lead: The arrest of Rosa Parks sparked a 381-day boycott that made non-violent mass protest a national force.

blocks.text: On 1 December 1955 **Rosa Parks** was arrested in Montgomery, Alabama, for refusing to give up her bus seat to a white passenger. The black community responded by boycotting the city's buses for over a year, walking and car-pooling instead. A young minister, **Dr Martin Luther King Jr**, was chosen to lead the campaign.

items: The boycott hurt the bus company financially and drew national attention.
In late 1956 the Supreme Court ruled bus segregation in Montgomery unconstitutional.
It launched King as a leader and proved the power of disciplined, non-violent mass action.

media.alt: An empty city bus beside a line of people walking, symbolising the boycott.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='20' y='58' width='120' height='48' rx='6' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='30' y='66' width='22' height='18' fill='#dcd3c2'/><rect x='58' y='66' width='22' height='18' fill='#dcd3c2'/><rect x='86' y='66' width='22' height='18' fill='#dcd3c2'/><rect x='114' y='66' width='18' height='18' fill='#dcd3c2'/><circle cx='48' cy='110' r='10' fill='#2f2f2f'/><circle cx='116' cy='110' r='10' fill='#2f2f2f'/><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='168' cy='72' r='9'/><rect x='161' y='84' width='14' height='30'/><circle cx='196' cy='68' r='9'/><rect x='189' y='80' width='14' height='34'/><circle cx='222' cy='72' r='9'/><rect x='215' y='84' width='14' height='30'/></g><rect x='16' y='124' width='210' height='5' fill='#c8b89a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain why Montgomery matters as a turning point: it moved the struggle from courtrooms to communities. Note Parks was an experienced NAACP activist, not simply a tired passenger - her stand was principled and organised. The boycott's success depended on ordinary people's sacrifice for over a year. Draw the comparison to South Africa's bus boycotts (Alexandra 1957) and the Defiance Campaign - similar tactics of economic pressure and mass discipline.

slides.heading: The tactics of non-violent resistance
slides.lead: Inspired by Gandhi and the Black church, activists used disciplined, peaceful direct action to expose injustice.

blocks.heading: Sit-ins
blocks.text: From 1960, students occupied 'whites-only' lunch counters, starting in Greensboro, North Carolina.

blocks.heading: Freedom Rides
blocks.text: In 1961 mixed-race riders challenged segregation on interstate buses, facing mob violence.

blocks.heading: Marches & boycotts
blocks.text: Organisations such as the SCLC and SNCC led marches, voter drives and economic boycotts.

slides.notes: Unpack the philosophy: non-violence was both a moral commitment and a strategy. By remaining peaceful while being attacked, protesters won public sympathy and exposed the brutality of segregation - especially on television. Introduce the key organisations: the SCLC (Southern Christian Leadership Conference, King), SNCC (Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee) and CORE (Congress of Racial Equality). Emphasise the leading role of young people and students - a point of comparison with South African student movements later in the unit.

slides.heading: Birmingham and the power of television
slides.lead: Images of police dogs and fire hoses turned on peaceful protesters shocked the world.

blocks.text: In 1963 the SCLC targeted **Birmingham, Alabama**, one of the most segregated cities in the US. When police used dogs and high-pressure fire hoses against marchers - including children - television cameras carried the images across America and the world, building pressure on the federal government to act.

items: King was jailed and wrote his famous 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' defending direct action.
The media made local injustice a national and international issue.
Public outrage helped push President Kennedy to propose civil rights legislation.

media.alt: A television set broadcasting an image of a marching protester.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='40' y='36' width='120' height='84' rx='8' fill='#3a3a3a'/><rect x='52' y='46' width='96' height='64' rx='4' fill='#dcd3c2'/><g fill='#8a1f1f'><circle cx='96' cy='66' r='8'/><rect x='90' y='74' width='12' height='26'/></g><rect x='118' y='60' width='4' height='40' fill='#5c3a1e'/><path d='M118 60 h18 v10 h-18 Z' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='150' y='120' width='10' height='16' fill='#3a3a3a'/><rect x='40' y='120' width='10' height='16' fill='#3a3a3a'/><path d='M96 36 L78 18 M96 36 L114 18' stroke='#3a3a3a' stroke-width='3'/></svg>

slides.notes: Focus on the role of the media as a weapon of civil society. The movement deliberately sought confrontation in places where a violent response would be caught on camera, converting local repression into national political pressure. Read or paraphrase a line from the 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' on the moral duty to disobey unjust laws. Compare with how images of Sharpeville (1960) and later Soweto (1976) mobilised world opinion against apartheid.

slides.eyebrow: The high point of the movement - 28 August 1963
slides.heading: The March on Washington and 'I Have a Dream'
slides.lead: About 250,000 people gathered peacefully at the Lincoln Memorial to demand jobs and freedom.

blocks.text: I have a dream that my four little children will one day live in a nation where they will not be judged by the colour of their skin but by the content of their character.
blocks.cite: Dr Martin Luther King Jr, Washington DC, 28 August 1963

blocks.text: The **March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom** was the largest political gathering the US had seen. King's speech gave the movement a powerful moral vision and became one of the most famous speeches in history. The peaceful, dignified march strengthened the case for federal civil rights laws.

items: It united many civil rights, labour, church and student organisations.
It combined demands for legal equality with demands for economic justice and jobs.
It kept up the pressure that led to major legislation the following year.

slides.notes: Use this slide to consolidate the achievements of the non-violent phase. Note the march's twin demands - 'Jobs and Freedom' - so learners see the movement was about economic as well as legal equality, foreshadowing later Black Power critiques. Discuss why King's vision of an integrated, colour-blind America is significant, and set up the coming tension: some activists would argue that this dream came too slowly and asked too much patience of black Americans.

slides.heading: Civil Rights Act and Voting Rights Act
slides.lead: Years of protest produced landmark laws that dismantled legal segregation.

blocks.text: The **Civil Rights Act of 1964** outlawed discrimination based on race, colour, religion, sex or national origin, and banned segregation in public places and employment. After the brutal suppression of marchers at **Selma**, the **Voting Rights Act of 1965** outlawed the practices used to stop black citizens voting.

items: These laws were the movement's greatest legislative achievements.
Black voter registration in the South rose sharply after 1965.
Yet poverty, police violence and de facto segregation, especially in Northern cities, continued.

media.alt: A signed legal document with an official seal, and a ballot being placed in a box.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='24' y='24' width='90' height='112' rx='4' fill='#fff' stroke='#c8b89a' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='36' y='40' width='66' height='6' fill='#cdd6b5'/><rect x='36' y='54' width='66' height='6' fill='#cdd6b5'/><rect x='36' y='68' width='50' height='6' fill='#cdd6b5'/><circle cx='58' cy='104' r='14' fill='#e8a13a'/><path d='M46 118 q12 -10 24 0' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3' fill='none'/><rect x='140' y='78' width='60' height='50' rx='4' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='150' y='68' width='40' height='12' rx='2' fill='#1f3a24'/><rect x='158' y='44' width='24' height='30' fill='#fff' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2' transform='rotate(-10 170 59)'/></svg>

slides.notes: Stress cause and effect: sustained civil society pressure forced the state to change the law. But end on the crucial limitation that opens the next section - legal equality did not end poverty, unemployment, slum housing or police brutality, especially in Northern cities like the Watts area of Los Angeles, where a major uprising broke out in 1965. This gap between legal rights and lived reality is exactly what the Black Power movement would address.

slides.heading: From civil rights to Black Power, 1954-1968

blocks.alt: Timeline of major events from 1954 to 1968.

events.label: Brown v. Board of Education ends legal school segregation
Montgomery Bus Boycott begins after Rosa Parks's arrest
Civil Rights Act; Malcolm X breaks with the Nation of Islam
Voting Rights Act; Malcolm X assassinated
'Black Power' slogan raised; Black Panther Party founded
Martin Luther King Jr assassinated in Memphis

slides.notes: Use the timeline to mark the shift from the non-violent civil rights phase to the more militant Black Power phase around 1965-1966. Ask learners to notice how the assassinations of Malcolm X (1965) and King (1968) bracket this turn. This is the pivot of the lesson: from integration and non-violence towards self-reliance, black pride and, for some, armed self-defence.

slides.heading: The rise of Black Power
slides.lead: Frustrated by slow change and continuing violence, younger activists demanded self-reliance and black pride.

blocks.text: **Malcolm X** had already argued that black Americans should build their own power and defend themselves 'by any means necessary', rather than wait for white goodwill. In 1966 **Stokely Carmichael** of SNCC popularised the slogan **'Black Power'**, calling for black political and economic self-determination and pride in black identity.

items: The **Black Panther Party**, founded in 1966, combined armed self-defence with community programmes such as free breakfasts for children.
Black Power stressed black culture, history and independent institutions over integration.
Critics feared it divided the movement; supporters said it gave black Americans dignity and control.

media.alt: A raised clenched fist, the symbol of the Black Power movement.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 180 160' role='img'><rect width='180' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><g fill='#2f2f2f'><rect x='78' y='96' width='24' height='52' rx='8'/><rect x='66' y='70' width='48' height='34' rx='10'/><rect x='66' y='58' width='11' height='20' rx='4'/><rect x='80' y='52' width='11' height='26' rx='4'/><rect x='94' y='56' width='11' height='22' rx='4'/><rect x='105' y='64' width='14' height='18' rx='6'/></g><circle cx='90' cy='40' r='4' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Present Black Power fairly and analytically, not as simply 'the violent movement'. Explain it as a response to real limits: legal rights had not ended poverty or police violence. Distinguish rhetoric of self-defence from indiscriminate violence. Note the Black Panthers' significant community work. This is a good point to weigh strengths and weaknesses: Black Power built pride and self-reliance but faced heavy state repression (the FBI's COINTELPRO) and internal divisions. [SME] Confirm the level of detail on the Black Panthers and COINTELPRO appropriate for the class.

slides.heading: Two responses, one struggle
slides.lead: Civil Rights and Black Power disagreed on methods and goals but shared the aim of black liberation.

items: Non-violence and peaceful protest.
Goal of integration into American society.
Worked through courts, laws and alliances with white supporters.
Leaders such as Martin Luther King Jr.
Self-defence and self-reliance.
Goal of black pride, identity and self-determination.
Built independent black institutions and communities.
Figures such as Malcolm X and Stokely Carmichael.

slides.notes: Guide learners to evaluate rather than simply describe. Ask: which approach achieved more, and by what measure? Non-violence won landmark laws and world sympathy; Black Power reshaped black identity and self-image and addressed economic and psychological oppression the laws left untouched. Encourage learners to see them as overlapping and sequential rather than as simple opposites - many activists moved between them. This comparison is prime CAPS essay material.

slides.heading: Connections with South Africa
slides.lead: The American struggle inspired, and was inspired by, resistance in South Africa.

blocks.text: The two struggles influenced each other directly. In 1962 **Chief Albert Luthuli**, President of the ANC and a Nobel Peace laureate, and **Martin Luther King Jr** issued a joint 'Appeal for Action Against Apartheid'. Later, the ideas of Black Power and black pride helped shape South Africa's **Black Consciousness Movement (BCM)**, led by **Steve Biko** through the South African Students' Organisation (SASO).

items: Both movements used civil society - churches, students and community groups - against racist states.
Black Consciousness echoed Black Power's message that black South Africans must free themselves psychologically before they could be free politically.
A key difference: South Africa's apartheid state gave activists almost no legal or voting channels, so peaceful protest was met with banning, imprisonment and death.

media.alt: Two hands clasped across a globe, linking the United States and South Africa.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='120' cy='80' r='52' fill='#a8bcae'/><path d='M92 52 q18 10 6 26 q-14 10 -2 26 M150 54 q-16 12 -4 26 q12 12 2 26' fill='none' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='4'/><g fill='#8a1f1f'><path d='M60 84 q26 -6 50 6 q-24 12 -50 6 Z'/><path d='M180 84 q-26 -6 -50 6 q24 12 50 6 Z'/></g><rect x='114' y='82' width='12' height='10' fill='#5c1414'/></svg>

slides.notes: This slide anchors the 'comparative civil society' requirement of the CAPS topic. Emphasise both the shared inspiration and the crucial structural difference: American activists could appeal to a constitution, courts and the vote, while apartheid closed those doors, which pushed the ANC towards the armed struggle in 1961. [SME] Verify the depth of the claimed influence of US Black Power on the South African Black Consciousness Movement - historians agree there was influence (via Fanon, US Black Power writing and Black Theology), but confirm the wording is not overstated for the classroom.

slides.heading: Legacy of the movements
slides.lead: Together the movements ended legal segregation and transformed how black identity and rights were understood.

blocks.text: The Civil Rights Movement dismantled the legal framework of segregation and won voting rights, while Black Power reshaped black identity, culture and self-confidence. The assassination of **Martin Luther King Jr** on 4 April 1968 was a devastating blow, but the movements had permanently changed American society and inspired freedom struggles worldwide.

items: They showed the power of organised civil society to force a democratic state to change.
They left ongoing debates about how to close the gap between legal rights and real equality.
Their strategies and symbols, from mass marches to the raised fist, echoed across the globe.

slides.notes: Close the analysis by balancing achievement and unfinished business: legal equality was won, but economic inequality, segregated housing and police violence persisted - issues that still resonate today. Invite learners to reflect on what 'success' means for a protest movement. Reinforce the comparative frame: like the anti-apartheid struggle, these movements combined many tactics over time and drew strength from ordinary citizens organised in civil society.

slides.heading: Consolidation and questions
slides.lead: Check your understanding of civil society protest in the 1950s-1970s.

blocks.text: The US Civil Rights and Black Power movements were two connected responses to racial oppression: one seeking integration through non-violence, the other self-determination through black pride and self-reliance. Both relied on the organised action of ordinary citizens, and both had strong links to South Africa's own liberation struggle.

blocks.question: Explain how and why the strategy of the movement shifted from Civil Rights to Black Power during the 1960s.
Compare the aims and methods of Martin Luther King Jr and the Black Power movement. Which do you think was more effective, and why?
Identify two ways in which the US struggle was connected to civil society protest in South Africa.

slides.notes: Use the questions as written work or structured discussion; the mark allocations mirror CAPS source-based and extended-writing tasks. Encourage learners to argue a clear line in the comparison essay, using evidence for both sides. Remind them that 'more effective' has no single right answer - what matters is a supported argument. Link forward to the next topic on the Black Consciousness Movement and the road to democracy in South Africa.
